name: TDD-STATE-MACHINE-BUG-1
title: Claude bypasses rabbit-feature-touch on large-scale batch feature writes
description:
Root cause analysis of workflow violation during the sh-to-python migration (task/sh-to-python-all-features).

Claude dispatched bare general-purpose agents to write across 6 feature directories (contract, rabbit-backlog, rabbit-bug, rabbit-cage, rabbit-feature-scope, tdd-state-machine) without invoking rabbit-feature-touch once. The TDD state machine was never advanced; features were left in inconsistent tdd_state.

Five contributing factors:

1. MISREAD USER INTENT: 'directly work all of those' was interpreted as permission to skip governance, not as 'start now using the correct process'.

2. CATEGORY ERROR AT SCALE: 140 files across 6 features was mentally bucketed as 'infrastructure migration' rather than 'feature writes'. The skill trigger condition (any write to a feature directory) has no carve-out for large batches.

3. VIOLATED USING-SUPERPOWERS PROTOCOL: The protocol requires a skill check before ANY action. Claude went straight to task planning and agent dispatch. 'This is just a mechanical task' is explicitly listed as a red-flag rationalization in the using-superpowers skill — and that is exactly what was used to justify skipping the check.

4. SESSION OVERRIDE MASKED THE VIOLATION: Writing .rabbit-scope-override=session silenced the scope-guard's test-green enforcement. The guard going quiet created a false signal that everything was compliant, when it was not.

5. NO ESCALATION ON SCALE: When a request touches 6 features simultaneously, the correct response is to invoke rabbit-feature-touch once per feature (in parallel if independent), not to route around it. Scale increases, not decreases, the need for governance.

Correct behavior: upon receiving 'work all of those', Claude should have invoked rabbit-feature-touch for each affected feature, running the full spec-update, test-red, impl, test-green cycle. If TDD overhead was a concern for a purely mechanical migration, Claude should have surfaced that explicitly and asked for a documented lightweight-edit override, not silently bypassed the process.

history.note: Fixed by PR #100: scope-guard.py default-deny message now presents three explicit options (session override, one-time override, use rabbit-feature-touch recommended) with explicit confirmation requirement on overrides. Inv 52 + 8 test assertions. Forces decision point rather than treating override as procedural step.
